questions.question: During the session of the house of parliament, permission of which authority is necessary to arrest a member of parliament?
questions.explanation: No person (either a member or outsider) can be arrested, and no legal process (civil or criminal) can be served within the precincts of the House without the permission of the presiding officer.

questions.question: NITI Aayog promotes which among the following characters in Indian Polity?
questions.explanation: NITI Aayog replaces the Planning Commission to promote cooperative federalism.

questions.question: Which among the following fundamental rights is available to foreigners also?
options: Right to freedom of speech & expression
questions.explanation: Article 21 A declares that the State shall provide free and compulsory education to all children of the age of six to fourteen years. It is applicable to both citizens and foreigners.

questions.question: Which among the following schedules can be amended only with consent of the state legislatures?
questions.explanation: Seventh schedule deals with division of power between states and centre. So, it can be amended only with consent of half of the state legislatures.

questions.question: What is the maximum period for which President's Rule can be imposed in a state?
questions.explanation: President's rule can be extended for a maximum period of three years with the approval of the Parliament, every six months.

questions.question: Instrument of Accession is related to which state/Union Territory?
questions.explanation: State of Jammu and Kashmir (J&K) became independent on 15 August 1947. The ruler of the state decided to accede the state to India. Accordingly, the 'Instrument of Accession of Jammu and Kashmir to India' was signed by Pandit Jawaharlal Nehru and Maharaja Hari Singh on 26 October 1947.

questions.question: Supreme court decides on disputes related to election to which of the following offices?
questions.explanation: All doubts and disputes in connection with election of the President are inquired into and decided by the Supreme Court whose decision is final.

questions.question: To initiate civil proceedings against Governor of a State, a notice must be given how many months prior to that?
questions.explanation: After giving two months' notice, civil proceedings can be instituted against Governor during his term of office in respect of his personal acts.

questions.question: Tribal Welfare portfolio is NOT mandatory in which among the following states?
questions.explanation: There should be a Tribal Welfare minister in the states of Chhattisgarh, Jharkhand, Madhya Pradesh and Odisha appointed by Governor of the respective states.

questions.question: To which among the following constitutional posts, the appointing authority and removing authority is different?
questions.explanation: The chairman and members of a SPSC are appointed by the governor, they can be removed only by the President.

questions.question: Who among the following is NOT allowed to undertake private legal practices even after their retirement?
questions.explanation: The retired judges of the Supreme Court are prohibited from pleading or acting in any Court or before any authority within the territory of India. This ensures that they do not favour anyone in the hope of future favour.

questions.question: President has no pardoning power with respect to _______?
options: Award of death sentence by a court-martial
Award of death sentence under state law
questions.explanation: Only Governor can pardon, reprieve, respite, remit, suspend or commute the punishment or sentence of any person convicted of any offence against a state law.

questions.question: Governor of a state must discharge his duties with the aid and advice of ______?
questions.explanation: Article 163 provides for a council of ministers with the chief minister at the head to aid and advise the governor in the exercise of his functions except the discretionary ones.

questions.question: Which among the following procedure ends a session of Parliament?
questions.explanation: Prorogation is issued to terminate a session of Lok Sabha. The house gets into recess period till next summoning.

questions.question: Who presides over a Joint sitting of houses of parliament?
questions.explanation: Speaker of Lok Sabha presides over a joint setting of the two Houses of Parliament. Such a sitting is summoned to settle a deadlock between the two Houses on a bill.

questions.question: Which among the following is NOT a constitutional body?
questions.explanation: Originally the Central Vigilance Commission was neither a constitutional body nor a statutory body. In September 2003, the Parliament enacted a law conferring statutory status on the CVC.

questions.question: Who among the following is responsible for the conduct of elections at constituency levels in General elections?
questions.explanation: The Returning Officer of a Parliamentary or assembly constituency is responsible for the conduct of elections in the Parliamentary or assembly constituency concerned.

questions.question: What is the maximum number of directors can a cooperative society have?
questions.explanation: The board of cooperative society shall consist of such number of directors as may be provided by the state legislature. But, the maximum number of directors of a co-operative society shall not exceed twenty-one.

questions.question: A nominated member of parliament can join a party within what period of taking the seat without attracting charges of defection?
questions.explanation: A nominated member of a House becomes disqualified for being a member of the House if he joins any political party after the expiry of six months from the date on which he takes his seat in the House. This means that he may join any political party within six months of taking his seat in the House without inviting this disqualification.

questions.question: Which is the last Union Territory to be created?
questions.explanation: Chandigarh was the last Union Territory to be created in 1966. At present, there are seven Union Territories. (1) Andaman and Nicobar Islands - 1956, (2) Delhi - 1956, (3) Lakshadweep - 1956, (4) Dadra and Nagar Haveli - 1961, (5) Daman and Diu - 1962, (6) Puducherry - 1962, and (7) Chandigarh - 1966.

questions.question: A qualified person can hold the office of chairman of National Human Rights Commission till he/she attains _____ age?
questions.explanation: The chairman and members hold office for a term of five years or until they attain the age of 70 years, whichever is earlier.

questions.question: Who among the following is NOT on the recommendation committee for the appointment of members of Central Information Commission?
options: Union Cabinet minister nominated by PM
questions.explanation: They are appointed by the President on the recommendation of a committee consisting of the Prime Minister as Chairperson, the Leader of Opposition in the Lok Sabha and a Union Cabinet Minister nominated by the Prime Minister.

questions.question: A judge of a High Court can be removed by President only when a motion for that purpose is presented by ________?
questions.explanation: The President can issue the removal order for Judge of a High Court only after an address by the Parliament has been presented to her/him in the same session for such removal.

questions.question: Which authority can establish a Joint State Public Service Commission?
questions.explanation: The Parliament can establish a Joint State Public Service Commission (JSPSC) for two or more states on the request of the state legislatures concerned. The chairman and members of the JSPSC are appointed by the president.

questions.question: Which authority can make statutory grants to states?
questions.explanation: Article 275 empowers the Parliament to make grants to the states which need financial assistance and not to every state.

questions.question: Which among the following emergency cannot be proclaimed by simple majority in the parliament?
questions.explanation: Every resolution approving the proclamation of National emergency or its continuance must be passed by either House of Parliament by a special majority.

questions.question: Constitution provides for how many types of funds for the Union Government?
questions.explanation: The Constitution of India provides for the following three kinds of funds for the Central government: 1. Consolidated Fund of India (Article 266) 2. Public Account of India (Article 266) 3. Contingency Fund of India (Article 267).

questions.question: Which authority can legislate on residuary subjects?
questions.explanation: The primary function of Parliament is to make laws for the governance of the country. It has exclusive power to make laws on the subjects enumerated in the Union List and on the residuary subjects (that is, subjects not enumerated in any of the three lists).

questions.question: President's pocket veto power can be exercised over a bill for a maximum period of_______?
questions.explanation: The President can exercise pocket veto power as the Constitution does not prescribe any time-limit within which he has to take the decision with respect to a bill presented to him for his assent.

questions.question: Supreme Court hears appeals in which language?
questions.explanation: Supreme Court hears only those who petition or appeal in English. The Parliament has not made any provision for the use of Hindi in the Supreme Court.
